text.title: framework-decomposition-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for framework-decomposition-patterns. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.rationale: God models couple business logic with persistence. Limit scope to domain properties and basic validations; extract behavior to services.
Services own writes and side effects. Single-responsibility: each service = one business operation or closely related set of operations.
Controllers are dispatch and HTTP layer only. Delegate business logic to services; keep HTTP concerns (status codes, headers) separate from domain.
Components should focus on rendering and event handling. Extract large state-management or data-fetching logic to hooks or services.
Hooks encapsulate stateful logic. Large hooks fragment the component tree and make reuse harder.

rule.statement: Test: 300 lines max (target 100-150 lines). Split into multiple test classes/modules if larger.
rule.rationale: Large test files make it hard to find a specific test. Organize by subject under test.

rule.statement: Before running an LLM-assisted feature on a module, check if any file in the touch path exceeds 200 lines. If yes, extract first, then run the feature.
rule.rationale: The decomposition investment pays back immediately on the first LLM task. Running the feature first on a fat file embeds the debt deeper as new code is layered on top.

rule.statement: Make one commit per extraction (one service, one selector, one hook). Never combine "extract service" + "rename" in one diff.
rule.rationale: Agents lose blame trail when extractions are combined. Reviewers (and future agents) cannot identify which change introduced a regression. Single-extraction commits enable surgical revert.

rule.statement: In Django, extract selectors.py (pure queryset reads, no writes) and services.py (business logic, writes, side effects) from views.py. Keep views to URL-dispatch and HTTP layer only.
rule.rationale: Django Styleguide (HackSoft) pattern: fat views are the most common Django debt source. Service+Selector split gives agents a clear mental model: "does this touch the database? Is it a read or write?"

rule.statement: In Rails, use service objects for multi-step business actions only — not for trivial CRUD. Return Result.success(value) / Result.failure(error) not exceptions.
rule.rationale: Service objects that wrap simple ActiveRecord calls fight the framework. Result types let agents handle success/failure paths reliably without rescuing exceptions. One service = one business operation.

rule.statement: In Laravel, use single-class Actions (from laravel-actions package or plain invokable classes) for each business operation. Use Form Requests or typed DTOs as the input contract.
rule.rationale: Actions map 1:1 to business operations, giving agents a clear entry point. DTOs typed with constructor promotion prevent silent field drift in service signatures.

rule.statement: In React, extract a custom hook when: the stateful logic is used in 2+ components, OR the hook body exceeds 30 LOC. Do not split JSX into 3-line components — composition has overhead.
rule.rationale: Over-extraction fragments the component tree without reducing complexity. Hook extraction yields returns only when logic is genuinely reused or genuinely large. Prefer colocation.

rule.statement: Creating wrappers without behavior: UserService.get() wrapping User.objects.get(). If the selector just forwards, it's not needed.
rule.rationale: Wrappers add indirection cost without benefit. Use selectors when filtering, joining, prefetching, or caching — behavior matters.

rule.statement: Over-extracting components: splitting one component into five pieces, none of which are reused. Composition has overhead — nesting 5 small components is harder to follow than one 80-line component.
rule.rationale: Extract only when reuse signal exists or complexity genuinely requires isolation (e.g., complex modal logic, reusable form field).

rule.statement: Service objects for simple CRUD in Rails or Laravel. If the framework idiom handles it in 2 lines, don't wrap it in a service.
rule.rationale: Services hide behavior behind indirection. Visible CRUD code is simpler to reason about than delegating to a service that does nothing but wrap.

rule.statement: Mixing reads and writes in a single service. Service.get_and_update() is a red flag: reads and writes have different caching and invalidation concerns.
rule.rationale: Separate selectors (reads) from services (writes) so agents can reason about side effects and cache without reading both paths.
